assignments.title: Week 11 Clean & Comment Your Code: /update-one-animal API Endpoint & Helper Function
assignments.description_html:
This week, you will clean and comment your code for the /update-one-animal API Endpoint and Helper Function for the Animals API project. 

 

Overview

  • During Block A on Thursdays, you’ll review, clean, and organize the code you’ve written that week
  • You’ll add helpful comments that explain what your code does and why
  • Refer to this guide: Cleaning & Commenting Your Code Best Practices.

 

Why this matters

  • It helps you understand your own logic more deeply
  • Clean, readable code is easier to debug and maintain
  • Commenting your code is a professional standard on dev teams
  • Good documentation supports your peers and your future self

 

How to turn this in

  • Please submit the Github link to your Recipe API's index.js file in your nodejs-workbook Github repo. Your instructor will review your code and comments in this file. 
  • You will also clean and comment the rest of your code, not just what you turn in to be graded
    • This is important practice even if it’s not all being graded — it reinforces strong coding habits and supports your learning.

 

How to get the correct Github file link

  1. Open your repo on GitHub.

  2. Navigate to the file you cleaned and commented.

  3. Click the file to view it.

  4. Copy the URL from your browser's address bar (it should end in the filename, like .../MyComponent.jsx).

  5. Paste that link into the assignment submission.

💡 This ensures your instructor can quickly find and review exactly the file you're presenting.

assignments.title: Week 3 Explain Your Code: Storing & Retrieving Form Data from Local Storage
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will present their code for getting the saved countries data from local storage. 

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Design to Data: Airtable — Resourcefull
assignments.description_html:
Introduction

In this project, you will continue to learn how to analyze web page designs, identify the data needed to support the functionality, and create database tables with appropriate data types, primary keys, and relationships. 

  • Skills Learned:
    1. Analyzing UI/UX designs to determine data requirements.
    2. Structuring and normalizing data into database tables.
    3. Identifying primary keys, foreign keys, and data types.

You will practice these skills by applying them to the Resourcefull app. 

 

Structuring Data into Tables

Your goal is to analyze the Resourcefull Figma designs, organize the data fields into tables on Airtable, and identify relationships between the data tables.

You should think through the data models for the first 4 screens in the Figma designs:

  1. Provider Eligibility
  2. Create Account
  3. Organization Profile
  4. Program Profile

Create Database Tables in Airtable

  • Create tables for the data fields you identify. These should be created as separate tabs in an Airtable instance
  • Each table should include:
    • Table Name
    • Data Fields as columns (with data types)
    • Primary Key
    • One row of data (if there is example data in the Figma design, you can use that. Or if there is no example data, you can use your imagination to make up the data) 
  • Here are some questions you should take into consideration while you are building out your data models:
    • What is the data that I want to store?
    • What is the relationship betweent the data in the various tables?
    • How do I want to connect a user to an organization?

To help you visualize the data model, you can build out an ERD diagram if you'd like (this is optional). Here's a free tool for making ERD diagrams: https://www.smartdraw.com/entity-relationship-diagram/er-diagram-tool.htm 

 

How to turn this in

Turn in the Airtable link.

assignments.title: Week 3 Daily Work Journal
assignments.description_html:
Overview

In this course, you’ll be building projects over several days. To help you stay organized and plan ahead, we’ve created a Daily Work Journal. Use it to track what you worked on, what’s next, and any questions or blockers.

Each day, make an entry that answers the required questions:

  • What I worked on today
  • What I need to work on tomorrow
  • Questions or blockers 

Optional: Reflect on today's biggest win. 

 

If you are absent or it's a holiday

  • Full-day absence: Mark it clearly in your journal to receive credit.

  • Partial-day absence: Complete your journal as best you can. Note when you were absent (e.g., “Absent during Block A”).

  • Holiday: No entry required. Just mark the day as a holiday.

 

No late work journal entries

Entries must be completed the same day. Late submissions will not be accepted. The journal only takes a few minutes—please stay on top of it!

 

Incomplete Assignments List (Weekly)

At the end of each week, fill out the Incomplete Assignments section in your journal. List any incomplete assignments you still need to complete.

To complete this section:

  1. Go to your Grades

  2. For each incomplete assignment, add a checkbox with the Week number and assignment name.

If you are behind on 6 or more assignments, your instructor may reach out to review your action plan. 

 

What if I'm all caught up? 

That's fantastic! 🎉

In the Incomplete Assignments section, simply write something like:

“I am all caught up on my assignments.”

 

How to turn this in

Each Friday, submit the link to your Daily Work Journal.
Your instructor will check:

  • Your entries from Monday through Friday

  • Your Incomplete Assignments List for awareness and accountability

assignments.title: Week 6 Explain Your Code: SQL Popcorn 🍿
assignments.description_html:
Overview

In this Explain Your Code session, we're going to switch things up a bit!

Instead of having presentations, we will play a game called SQL Popcorn. We'll look at a database together and each student will write a SQL query (or two!) on the spot. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

How it'll work

  • Students will take turns completing challenges by writing SQL commands.
  • As each student explains their SQL command out loud, the instructor will type it into DB Fiddle and run it.
  • When the student successfully completes a challenge, they will popcorn to the next person to do the next challenge.
  • If they get stuck, they can either ask a classmate for help or try a different challenge, depending on how close they are to the solution.

 

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit. The instructor will give you a database and ask you to write some SQL queries on the spot. 

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

Command  What it does 
ls
 List the contents of the current folder 
pwd
 Print Working Directory — Shows the file path from the root folder to the current folder 
cd foldername
 Change Directory  — Move to the specified folder 
cd ..
 Change Directory — Move to the parent folder  
cd ~

assignments.description_html:
This week, you will clean and comment your code for the /update-one-animal API Endpoint and Helper Function for the Animals API project. 

 

Overview

  • During Block A on Thursdays, you’ll review, clean, and organize the code you’ve written that week
  • You’ll add helpful comments that explain what your code does and why
  • Refer to this guide: Cleaning & Commenting Your Code Best Practices.

 

Why this matters

  • It helps you understand your own logic more deeply
  • Clean, readable code is easier to debug and maintain
  • Commenting your code is a professional standard on dev teams
  • Good documentation supports your peers and your future self

 

How to turn this in

  • Please submit the Github link to your Recipe API's index.js file in your nodejs-workbook Github repo. Your instructor will review your code and comments in this file. 
  • You will also clean and comment the rest of your code, not just what you turn in to be graded
    • This is important practice even if it’s not all being graded — it reinforces strong coding habits and supports your learning.

 

How to get the correct Github file link

  1. Open your repo on GitHub.

  2. Navigate to the file you cleaned and commented.

  3. Click the file to view it.

This Part 2 exercise is a continuation of the following assignment: Morning Warmup: Pokemon API Call — Part 1 — Listing All PokemonPlease complete Part 1 before doing Part 2.

assignments.title: Warm Up: Adding More Programming Language API Endpoints
assignments.description_html:
Add the following 3 API endpoints to your 9-programming-languages-api project in the intro-nodejs-workbook, where you have already started building a REST API for a programming languages app using PostgreSQL as our database and Node.js (JavaScript). 

1. /get-all-languages/sort-by-year

This API endpoint should return a list of all the programming languages, sorted by released_year. 

2. /get-all-languages/sort-by/:column

This API endpoint should return a list of all the programming languages, sorted by a column specified by the user, in ascending order. 

3. /search-languages-by-name/:name

This API endpoint should return a list of all the programming languages that match the search term. For example, if the user searches the name "Java", the API endpoint should return two languages, Java and Javascript.

assignments.title: Countries App - Version 2 — Complete!
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Create Version 2 of your Countries API Project, which builds on the Version 1 of the Countries API app by saving data in the browser using Local Storage. Your application will now remember:

  • the Form data
  • Saved Countries data
  • How many times each country has been viewed

By storing persistent data, your app will become more personal, dynamic, and interactive!

Requirements

Refer to the Github repo for project requirements and the full instructions: https://github.com/AnnieCannons/countries-api-project-may-2025/tree/main/version-2

Your Version 2 should save the following data to local storage: 

  • Form data 
    • The user's form data should be stored in local storage (including name, email, country, and bio)
    • As a user, when I submit the form, my data should be saved.
    • As a user, when I come back to the website, if I have already submitted the form, I should see "Welcome, [my name]!"
  • Saved Countries data 
    • The user's saved countries should be stored in local storage.
    • As a user, when I click on Save in an individual country card, I should be able to see that country's card in my Saved Countries page
    • As a user, on my Saved Countries page, I should be able to see all of the Saved Countries.
    • As a user, I cannot save a country more than once. If I try to save a country that has already been saved, then nothing will happen and it will not be saved again.
  • Count data (how many times a user views a specific country) 
    • When a user clicks on a Country Card from the Home page and goes to an Individual Country Page, the click should be added to local storage.
    • As a user, when I open an Individual Country Page, I should be able to see how many times the user has clicked this country in the Count section.

 

How to turn this in

In the text area on this assignment, turn in two links: 

1. The link to your Github repo

2. The link to your Netlify deployment of your Version 2

assignments.title: Week 8 Explain Your Partner's Code: Dog Age Calculator
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will explain someone else's dogAgeCalculator.js code. 

You will know whose code you will present right before your presentation. To help the person presenting your code, please make sure your code is clean and organized! 

 

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Data Models for Countries App & Wix Cookie Shop
assignments.description_html:
In this exercise, you'll design data models for two different web applications by identifying the data that needs to be stored on the backend.

Applications that store and display data — like your Countries App — need a clear plan for how information will be saved in a database.

Your task is to think through what that structure would look like using Google Sheets to design your models.

📝 Your task

You'll create data models (tables) in Google Sheets for these two applications:

  1. Your Countries App

  2. Wix Cookie Shop (based on the Wix Cookie Shop page)

For each application, there are 3 tables and their column names provided for you. Your task is to: 

  • Choose appropriate data types for each column (e.g., VARCHAR, INTEGER, BOOLEAN, DATE).

  • Specify which column is the primary key for each table.

  • Apply UNIQUE or NOT NULL constraints to the columns where appropriate. 

  • Add 3 sample rows of data per table to demonstrate what will be stored.

 

To start, make a copy of this Google Sheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1dVmIaBqg0LDW2-qc3Z89q04ApGoo_fd6YjxqmsGR2zo/edit?gid=1118224607#gid=1118224607 

📚 Data Model 1: Countries API Project

Create 3 tables that would store the following:

  1. User Profiles — Info submitted through the form

  2. Saved Countries — Which countries have been saved by users

  3. Country View Counts — How many times each country has been viewed

⚠️ Important Note: Match Your Countries App's Data Model to the Countries API 

Your Countries API project's data model should use the exact same key names as those in the Version 2 API documentation.

Because you’ve already built your Version 3 to send and receive data in a specific structure, it’s essential that your data model matches that structure — field by field.

This is important because your data model is the blueprint for your database, which will eventually be used by your API, so your tables' column names need to match the keys your API expects.

🍪 Data Model 2: Wix Cookie Shop

Create 3 tables that would store the following:

  1. Products — All cookies and treats for sale

  2. Orders — Each order placed, including product info and quantity

  3. Customers — Info about people who place orders

assignments.title: Countries App — Version 2 — Complete!
assignments.description_html:
Overview

In Version 2 of your Countries API project, you’ll upgrade your project to connect with a real backend!

Instead of storing data in local storage, you'll now store and retrieve data from a backend that’s provided for you. This version introduces how a frontend communicates with a backend using HTTP requests.

You’ll use the Fetch API to send and receive data — a critical skill for building real-world apps.

Coming up in Version 3, you'll go even further and build your own backend from scratch!

Requirements for Version 2

Refer to the Github repo for all the project requirements and full instructions:
Version 2 Instructions — Countries App

Your project should use the Fetch API to interact the backend. You’ll be storing and retrieving three types of data from the backend that’s been provided to you:

  1. Form data
    • As a user, when I submit the form, my data should be stored in the backend
    • As a user, if I have already submitted the form, I should see "Welcome, [my name]!" instead of the form.
  2. Saved Countries data
    • As a user, on my Saved Countries page, I should be able to see all of the Saved Countries.
    • As a user, when I click the Save button in an individual country's page, I should be able to see that country's card in my Saved Countries page
  3. View Count data
    • As a user, when I open a country's page, I should be able to see how many times the user has viewed this country.
    • Every time I view a country's page, its view count should go up by 1.

assignments.title: HackerRank SQL (Basic) Skill Certification
assignments.description_html:
Now that you've been steadily leveling up in SQL, it's time to get certified! 🏆✨

You will take this SQL (Basic) skill certification test on HackerRank: https://www.hackerrank.com/skills-verification/sql_basic 

The test has 2 questions and a 30 minute time limit.

If you pass, you will receive a HackerRank certificate with your name on it. This certificate is proof of the hard work you've put into learning this new programming language, and it's something you can show off on your portfolio, Github, or LinkedIn profiles! 

 

Preparing for the exam

  • Before you take the exam, we recommend you prepare yourself by working through some practice problems on these sites: 
    • SQL Bolt
      • Beginner level
      • Explains SQL concepts in a friendly manner, as if you've never written any SQL before.
      • Great place to start for general review!
      • Each lesson has a handful of practice questions at the end. 
    • PostgreSQL Exercises
      • Intermediate level
      • Has 7 different SQL problem sets for different SQL concepts like joins and aggregate functions
      • When you complete a question in the problem set, there's an explanation at the bottom about why the answer works. Read these because they provide thorough information about how SQL works under the hood. 
      • We recommend completing the questions in these problem sets at minimum: 
        1. Basic (all 12 questions)
        2. Joins and Subqueries (first 4 questions)
        3. Aggregates (first 8 questions)

Tips before you start the exam

  • HackerRank provides the option to take a practice test before starting the actual test. We recommend doing the practice test first so you can get familiar with HackerRank's testing interface before diving in.
  • Once you start the test, remember to change the SQL database engine to PostgreSQL (it is MySQL by default). This is important because it affects how your code is interpreted. 

 

How to turn this in

Submit a screenshot with your test result. It doesn't matter whether you passed the challenge or not — we just need to see proof that you made your best attempt.

After you take the test, you will find your test result either in your HackerRank user profile, or in your email inbox. It may take up to 30 minutes for HackerRank to send you your test result upon submitting. 

If you don't pass the test, that is okay and nothing to worry about! Remember that SQL is a brand new skill that you have only been learning for a couple weeks. In 30 days you will be able to try again.

assignments.description_html:
Overview

  • During Block A on Thursdays, you’ll review, clean, and organize the code you’ve written that week
  • You’ll add helpful comments that explain what your code does and why
  • Refer to this guide: Cleaning & Commenting Your Code Best Practices.

 

Why this matters

  • It helps you understand your own logic more deeply
  • Clean, readable code is easier to debug and maintain
  • Commenting your code is a professional standard on dev teams
  • Good documentation supports your peers and your future self

 

How to turn this in

  • Please submit the Github link to your SavedCountries.jsx file in Version 2 of your Countries API app. Your instructor will review your code and comments in this file. 
  • You will also clean and comment the rest of your code, not just what you turn in to be graded
    • This is important practice even if it’s not all being graded — it reinforces strong coding habits and supports your learning.

 

How to get the correct Github file link

  1. Open your repo on GitHub.

  2. Navigate to the file you cleaned and commented.

  3. Click the file to view it.

  4. Copy the URL from your browser's address bar (it should end in the filename, like .../MyComponent.jsx).

assignments.title: Week 5 Explain Your Code: SQL Database Schema for Countries App
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will present their SQL code for their Countries API project. They'll go table by table and describe what they built and how it works.

For Each Table (users, saved_countries, country_counts), talk about:

1. The Table’s Schema (Structure)

    • What is this table for?

    • What columns did you create? What data type did you use for each column?

    • Which column is the primary key?

    • Example: “In the users table, I have user_id as the primary key and it’s a SERIAL because it auto-increments. I used TEXT for name and email because those are text values.”

2. The Table’s Query SQL

    • How do you insert a new row of data?

    • How do you select data from the table?

    • Did you use any filters like WHERE, or sort the data with ORDER BY?

    • Did you update or delete any rows?

    • If you wrote something more advanced like LIMIT, feel free to explain that too!

    • Example: “This query selects all the users: SELECT * FROM users. And this one finds the newest user: ORDER BY user_id DESC LIMIT 1.”

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in DB Fiddle. 
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.description_html:
Goals

  • We will learn how to upsert, or in other words, inserting with conflict handling. 
    • The term upsert is a combination of the words “update” and “insert.”
    • In the context of relational databases, an upsert is a database operation that will update an existing row if a specified value already exists in a table, and insert a new row if the specified value doesn’t already exist.

Steps

  1. Fork this DB Fiddle:
    https://www.db-fiddle.com/f/pv1SXdXSsHDLKyuWSuyjsr/56 
  2. In the left sidebar, in the Fiddle Description, write your name where it says "Your Name:"
  3. We will fill out all the SQL queries for the cats table in class together during lecture.
  4. Your task is to practice these lecture concepts by applying them to the country_counts table in the DB Fiddle. 
  5. After writing each SQL command, make sure to run your code to check for errors before moving on. 
  6. Click Update at the top to save your code!

assignments.title: Warm Up: Data Exercise 1 — Countries API, Wix Cookie Shop, Fireart Studio
assignments.description_html:
In this exercise, you're going to be looking at designs and the product you've already built — the Countries API project — and thinking about the data structure that you'd want to send to the backend. 

An application like the Countries API project needs to store some data in a database. So in this exercise, you will be thinking through what the data model would look like when information is stored in the database. 

You'll create sample data models for 3 applications:

  1. Your Countries API project
  2. Wix Cookie Shop 
  3. Fireart Studio

As you are thinking about how you'd store this data, ask yourself these questions: 

        • What is going to be coded on the frontend? 
        • What needs to be stored on the backend?
        • How might different objects relate to one another?
        • What are the different data types I should use?

Instructions for this assignment 

  1. Fork the CodePen: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/pen/JoPxGXy?editors=0010 

  2. Countries API Data: Build out 3 JavaScript collections (arrays of objects) that would store the following data for the information being collected in the Countries API application: 
    • Each User Profile's information that they submit via the form 
    • Each User's Saved Countries
    • The Country Count (which is how many times any user has viewed a specific country's page) 
  3. Wix Cookie Page: Build out 3 JavaScript collections (arrays of objects) that would store the data for this Wix Cookie Shop page. Take some time to study the designs and think through the data you would need to store for the following:
    • Products sold on the sites
    • Orders placed on the site
    • Customers who make a purchase on the site.
  4. FineArt Studio Page: Build out 1 JavaScript collection that would store the profile data from this FineArt Studio Page.

assignments.title: Week 7 Explain Your Code: Github Pull Request
assignments.description_html:
Overview

Each developer will present their code contribution to the collaborative SQL cheatsheet on Github. 

How to prepare for your presentation

  1. Make sure you understand the code you submit throughout this course

    • It's often easy to find code that works — but that’s not enough.
    • Our goal is for you to take the time to learn why the code works and how to write it yourself before turning it in.

  2.  Clean up your code

    • Rename any confusing variables or functions.
    • Remove unused code.
    • Organize your logic into clear, readable sections.
    • Aim for readability — your code should feel like reading a well-organized newspaper: easy to scan, logical, and understandable at a glance.

  3. Add helpful comments

    • Write comments to remind yourself what each section of the code is doing and why.
    • This not only helps you during your presentation, but also for future review. 

Why this matters

This exercise is about talking through your code. It will help you:

  • Deepen your understanding
  • Improve how you communicate as a developer
  • Build your confidence when sharing work with others

You don’t have to be perfect. Mistakes and stumbles are part of learning! We’ll support each other and give helpful feedback. The more you practice, the stronger your skills will become.

Presentation Logistics

  1. The instructor will randomly assign a presentation order (with room for preferences).
  2. Before presenting, take a few minutes to:
    • Hide private content (e.g., Slack DMs).
    • Close any unrelated tabs or programs.
    • Take a deep breath, grab some water or a tactile toy — whatever helps you feel grounded and confident.
    • Open your project in VS Code and the browser.
  3. Presentations will follow the finalized order.
  4. After each presentation:
    • We’ll have a brief Q&A.
    • The instructor may give feedback or ask a technical question.
    • Students are welcome to ask questions or share thoughts too.

Make-up option if you miss class

If you miss class, you may present your code to the instructor one-on-one during lab hours to receive credit.

How to turn this in

There’s nothing to submit. Once you present your code in class, your instructor will mark this assignment as complete.

assignments.title: Week 10 Daily Work Journal & Incomplete Assignments List
assignments.description_html:
Overview

In this course, you’ll be building projects over several days. To help you stay organized and plan ahead, we’ve created a Daily Work Journal. Use it to track what you worked on, what’s next, and any questions or blockers.

Each day, make an entry that answers the required questions:

  • What I worked on today
  • What I need to work on tomorrow
  • Questions or blockers 

Optional: Reflect on today's biggest win. 

 

If you are absent or it's a holiday

  • Full-day absence: Mark it clearly in your journal to receive credit.

  • Partial-day absence: Complete your journal as best you can. Note when you were absent (e.g., “Absent during Block A”).

  • Holiday: No entry required. Just mark the day as a holiday.

 

No late work journal entries

Entries must be completed the same day. Late submissions will not be accepted. The journal only takes a few minutes—please stay on top of it!

 

Incomplete Assignments List (Weekly)

At the end of each week, fill out the Incomplete Assignments section in your journal. List any incomplete assignments you still need to complete.

To complete this section:

  1. Go to your Grades

  2. For each incomplete assignment, add a checkbox with the Week number and assignment name.

If you are behind on 6 or more assignments, your instructor may reach out to review your action plan. 

 

What if I'm all caught up? 

That's fantastic! 🎉

In the Incomplete Assignments section, simply write something like:

“I am all caught up on my assignments.”

 

How to turn this in

Each Friday, submit the link to your Daily Work Journal.
Your instructor will check:

  • Your entries from Monday through Friday

  • Your Incomplete Assignments List for awareness and accountability
